summary: My grandfather, Arthur Kohlstrunk, grew up in Boonton, New Jersey, as the son of Walter and Olga. He often joked about how the family names became more Americanized with each generation. Arthur shared many stories about his childhood in a diverse immigrant neighborhood and wished he could remember more German to connect with his roots.

Walter Kohlstrunk, my great-grandfather, immigrated to the United States from Germany in 1913 under unusual circumstances. He had served in the German army from 1910 to 1912 but hated it and left to work as a steward on a steamship. When World War I broke out, he was docked in New York Harbor and chose not to return to Germany, effectively starting a new life in New York City and later Boonton, New Jersey.

Germany was my great-grandfather Walter Kohlstrunk's homeland, where he served in the army before leaving in 1913. When World War I began, the German Kaiser recalled soldiers like Walter, but he chose to stay in the United States instead of returning. Sadly, this meant Walter never saw his family in Germany again.

The United States became my great-grandfather Walter Kohlstrunk's new home after he decided not to return to Germany in 1914. He settled in New York City and later moved to Boonton, New Jersey, where he raised his family with my great-grandmother Olga. The U.S. offered a fresh start, but it also meant Walter could never go back to Germany or see his family again.

The German Empire was the nation my great-grandfather Walter Kohlstrunk served under during his army years from 1910 to 1912. When World War I broke out, the Kaiser of the German Empire ordered all former soldiers to return, but Walter refused and stayed in the United States. This decision cut him off from the German Empire and his homeland forever.

New York Harbor was where my great-grandfather Walter Kohlstrunk's ship was docked when the German military recall order came during World War I. It was there that he made the life-changing decision to leave the ship and stay in the United States rather than return to Germany. This moment in New York Harbor marked the start of his new life in America.

After leaving his ship in New York Harbor, my great-grandfather Walter Kohlstrunk took up residence in New York City. It was his first home in the United States before moving to Boonton, New Jersey. New York City was where he began his new life free from the German army, married Olga, and started a family.

Boonton, New Jersey, was where my grandfather Arthur Kohlstrunk and his family lived after settling in the United States. It was a small town with a rich mix of immigrants from Germany, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, and Italy. My grandfather loved telling stories about growing up there, including how the neighborhood was a true melting pot and the funny mix-up involving a coal truck and the family name.

Sweden was one of the countries represented by immigrants living in Boonton, New Jersey, alongside my family. Growing up, my grandfather noticed how many cultures came together in that small town, including Swedish families. Although the Kohlstrunks initially stuck to their German roots, the presence of Swedes and others made Boonton a true melting pot.

Norway was another country of origin for many immigrants living in Boonton, New Jersey, where my grandfather grew up. The Norwegian families, along with others from Sweden, Denmark, and Italy, contributed to the diverse cultural fabric of the town. This mix of backgrounds shaped the neighborhood environment during my grandfather's childhood.

Denmark was among the European countries represented by immigrants in Boonton, New Jersey, where my grandfather Arthur Kohlstrunk was raised. The Danish families lived alongside Germans, Swedes, Norwegians, and Italians, creating a multicultural community. This diversity was a defining part of my grandfather's experience growing up in that town.

Italy was one of the countries from which many immigrants came to Boonton, New Jersey, where my grandfather's family lived. Italian families were part of the diverse group of European immigrants in the neighborhood. Although my family initially socialized mostly with other Germans, school helped bring the children of these different backgrounds together.
